searchParameters.q: what causes the aurora borealis colors

answerBox.snippet: In this case, the colours are caused by particles colliding with our atmosphere at an altitude of 60 miles or less. At these heights, it is a reaction with Nitrogen that causes the Aurora to be tinged with purple or blue and most commonly, you will see these colours towards the lower parts of the display.
snippetHighlighted: particles colliding with our atmosphere at an altitude of 60 miles or less

organic.title: What causes the Northern Lights? Aurora borealis explained
organic.snippet: The green we see in the aurora is characteristic of oxygen, while hints of purple, blue or pink are caused by nitrogen.

organic.title: Aurora colours: what causes them?
organic.snippet: The colours of the aurora borealis are caused when solar particles and gases in Earth's atmosphere collide.

peopleAlsoAsk.question: What is the rarest color in the aurora borealis?
peopleAlsoAsk.title: The Colours of the Northern Lights | Aurora Borealis - Perlan
peopleAlsoAsk.snippet: The rarest colour in the Northern Lights is a deep blue or purple, which is a result of ionized nitrogen at even lower altitudes. This colour is not seen as often as others and is a treat for those lucky enough to witness it.

peopleAlsoAsk.question: What is the strongest aurora color?
peopleAlsoAsk.title: Aurora - Wikipedia
peopleAlsoAsk.snippet: The change in auroral colour with altitude is therefore explained—oxygen red is predominant at high altitudes, followed by oxygen green and nitrogen blue/purple/red, then finally other hues of nitrogen blue/purple/red where particle collisions prevent oxygen from emissions. Green is the most common colour.

peopleAlsoAsk.question: What determines the color of the aurora borealis?
peopleAlsoAsk.title: Auroras - NASA Science
peopleAlsoAsk.snippet: The colors of an aurora come from atoms and molecules being energized by colliding with energetic particles in the upper atmosphere. The most common color is green, which is produced when oxygen is excited by electrons around 60 mi (100 km) above Earth's surface.Feb 4, 2025

peopleAlsoAsk.question: How rare is a red aurora borealis?
peopleAlsoAsk.title: Is anyone seeing rare RED aurora? Here's what causes it! It's EXTRA ...
peopleAlsoAsk.snippet: Red auroras are the second most rare type of Northern Lights (next to violet) because they occur at altitudes of about 240kms and higher! This means there are fewer oxygen molecules for the solar atoms to collide with.Nov 11, 2025
